en.extract:
The blue seedeater (Amaurospiza concolor) or Cabanis's seedeater is a species of bird in the cardinal family that is found in southern Mexico and Central America. The Ecuadorian seedeater (Amaurospiza aequatorialis) was formerly considered a subspecies of the blue seedeater. 


Taxonomy and systematics
The blue seedeater was formally described in 1861 by the German ornithologist Jean Cabanis based on a specimen that had been collected in Costa Rica. Cabanis placed the species in a new genus Amaurospiza and coined the binomial name  Amaurospiza concolor. The specific epithet is Latin meaning "uniform", "similar in colour" or "plain".
The  blue seedeater was formerly considered to be conspecific with the Ecuadorian seedeater. Most authorities now treat the Ecuadorian seedeateras a separate species. This is based largely on the results of molecular phylogenetic studies that were published in 2014 and 2023. Confusingly, as of January 2024, BirdLife International uses the scientific name A. moesta for blue seedeater. The BirdLife account encompasses what are now the blue, Ecuadorian, and blackish-blue seedeaters.
Two subspecies are recognised:

A. c. relicta (Griscom, 1934) – southwest Mexico
A. c. concolor Cabanis, 1861 – south Mexico, Belize, Honduras to Panama


Description
The blue seedeater is 11.5 to 14 cm (4.5 to 5.5 in) long and weighs 12 to 15 g (0.42 to 0.53 oz). The nominate male is entirely slate blue. The female's upperparts are cinnamon and the underparts tawny. The male A. c. relicta is more slaty (less blue) than the nominate and has black lores; the female is a paler cinnamon.
The songs and calls have been transcribed in several ways. A song from Chiapas, Mexico, is [1]. One from Panama is [2]. A call from Puebla, Mexico, is [3] and one from Panama is [4].


Distribution and habitat
The nominate blue seedeater is found from Chiapas in southern Mexico through Central America to western and central Panama. A. c. relicta is found in six southwestern Mexico states, Jalisco, Colima, Morelos, Puebla, Guerrero and Oaxaca. The species inhabits openings in and edges of humid montane and secondary forest. It most often is found at sites with bamboo. In northern Central America it ranges in elevation from 600 to 2,500 m (2,000 to 8,200 ft) but in Costa Rica inhabits the narrower range of 1,700 to 2,200 m (5,600 to 7,200 ft).


Behavior


Feeding
The blue seedeater's diet includes insects, seeds, and bamboo shoots.


Breeding
The only known blue seedeater nest was found in Mexico. It was a cup of coarse grass lined with finer grass placed in the fork of a slender branch. It contained two seedeater eggs and one of the brood parasite bronzed cowbird (Molothrus aeneus).


Status
The IUCN has not assessed the blue seedeater.


References


External links

Xeno-canto: audio recordings of Cabanis's seedeater

The '''blue seedeater''' ('''''Amaurospiza concolor''''') or '''Cabanis's seedeater''' is a [[species]] of bird in the [[Cardinalidae|cardinal]] [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] that is found in southern [[Mexico]] and [[Central America]]. The [[Ecuadorian seedeater]] (''Amaurospiza aequatorialis'') was formerly considered a [[subspecies]] of the blue seedeater. 

==Taxonomy and systematics==
The blue seedeater was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1861 by the German ornithologist [[Jean Cabanis]] based on a specimen that had been collected in Costa Rica. Cabanis placed the species in a new genus ''[[Amaurospiza]]'' and coined the [[binomial name]] '' Amaurospiza concolor''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Cabanis | first=Jean  | author-link=Jean Cabanis | year=1861 | title=Uebersicht der im Berliner Museum befindlichen vögel von Costa Rica. (Fortsetzung) | language=German | journal=Journal für Ornithologie | volume=9 | issue=49 | pages=3–11 [3]| url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/32902273 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1970 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=13 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=151 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14483386 }}</ref> The specific epithet is [[Latin]] meaning "uniform", "similar in colour" or "plain".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=116 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n116/mode/1up }}</ref>

The  blue seedeater was formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with the [[Ecuadorian seedeater]]. Most authorities now treat the Ecuadorian seedeateras a separate species. This is based largely on the results of [[molecular phylogenetic]] studies that were published in 2014 and 2023.<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Bryson | first1=R.W. Jr | last2=Chaves | first2=J. | last3=Smith | first3=B.T. | last4=Miller | first4=M.J. | last5=Winker | first5=K. | last6=Pérez-Emán | first6=J.L. | last7=Klicka | first7=K. | year=2014 | title=Diversification across the New World within the 'blue' cardinalids (Aves: Cardinalidae) | journal=Journal of Biogeography | volume=41 | pages=587–599 [590] | doi=10.1111/jbi.12218 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Areta | first1=J.I. | last2=Benítez Saldívar | first2=M.J. | last3=Lentino | first3=M. | last4=Miranda | first4=J. | last5=Ferreira | first5=M. | last6=Klicka | first6=J. | last7=Pérez-Emán | first7=J. | date=2023 | title=Phylogenetic relationships and systematics of the bamboo-specialist ''Amaurospiza'' blue seedeaters | journal=Ibis | volume=165 | issue=3 | pages=844-861 | doi=10.1111/ibi.13181}}</ref><ref name=ioc/><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Chesser | first1=R.T. | last2=Billerman | first2=S.M. | last3=Burns | first3=K.J. | last4=Cicero | first4=C. | last5=Dunn | first5=J.L. | last6=Hernández-Baños | first6=B.E. | last7=Jiménez | first7=R.A. | last8=Kratter | first8=A.W. | last9=Mason | first9=N.A. | last10=Rasmussen | first10=P.C. | last11=Remsen | first11=J.V.J. | last12=Winker | first12=K. | date=2023 | title=Sixty-fourth supplement to the American Ornithological Society's Check-list of North American Birds | journal=Ornithology | volume=140 | issue= | pages=1-11 | doi=10.1093/ornithology/ukad023 | doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{ cite web | last1=Clements | first1=J.F. | last2=Rasmussen | first2=P.C. | last3=Schulenberg | first3=T.S. | last4=Iliff | first4=M.J. | last5=Fredericks | first5=T.A. | last6=Gerbracht | first6=J.A. | last7=Lepage | first7=D. | last8=Spencer | first8=A. | last9=Billerman | first9=S.M. | last10=Sullivan | first10=B.L. | last11=Wood | first11=C.L. | year=2023 | title=The eBird/Clements checklist of birds of the world: v2023 | url=https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ | access-date=22 January 2024 }}</ref> Confusingly, as of January 2024, [[BirdLife International]] uses the scientific name ''A. moesta'' for blue seedeater. The BirdLife account encompasses what are now the blue, Ecuadorian, and blackish-blue seedeaters.<ref name=IUCN>{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2018 |title=''Amaurospiza moesta'' |volume=2018 |article-number=e.T105965570A132047632 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-2.RLTS.T105965570A132047632.en |access-date=22 January 2023}}</ref>

Two subspecies are recognised:<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=December 2023 | title=Cardinals, grosbeaks and 'tanager' allies | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/cardinals/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=22 January 2024 }}</ref>
* ''A. c. relicta'' ([[Ludlow Griscom|Griscom]], 1934) – southwest Mexico
* ''A. c. concolor'' [[Jean Cabanis|Cabanis]], 1861 – south Mexico, [[Belize]], Honduras to Panama

==Description==
The blue seedeater is {{cvt|11.5|to|14|cm}} long and weighs {{cvt|12|to|15|g}}. The nominate male is entirely slate blue. The female's upperparts are cinnamon and the underparts tawny. The male ''A. c. relicta'' is more slaty (less blue) than the nominate and has black lores; the female is a paler cinnamon.<ref name=BLSE-BOW>Ramos-Ordoñez, M. F., C. I. Rodríguez-Flores, C. A. Soberanes-González, M. d. C. Arizmendi, A. Jaramillo, and T. S. Schulenberg (2020). Blue Seedeater (''Amaurospiza concolor''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (T. S. Schulenberg, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.blusee1.01 retrieved 16 May 2021</ref>

The songs and calls have been transcribed in several ways.<ref name=BLSE-BOW/> A song from Chiapas, Mexico, is [https://www.xeno-canto.org/256629]. One from Panama is [https://www.xeno-canto.org/16165]. A call from Puebla, Mexico, is [https://www.xeno-canto.org/331207] and one from Panama is [https://www.xeno-canto.org/5536].

==Distribution and habitat==

The nominate blue seedeater is found from [[Chiapas]] in southern Mexico through Central America to western and central Panama. ''A. c. relicta'' is found in six southwestern Mexico states, [[Jalisco]], [[Colima]], [[Morelos]], [[Puebla]], [[Guerrero]] and [[Oaxaca]]. The species inhabits openings in and edges of humid [[montane forest|montane]] and [[secondary forest]]. It most often is found at sites with bamboo. In northern Central America it ranges in elevation from {{convert|600|to|2500|m|ft|abbr=on}} but in Costa Rica inhabits the narrower range of {{convert|1700|to|2200|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=BLSE-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Feeding===
The blue seedeater's diet includes insects, seeds, and bamboo shoots.<ref name=BLSE-BOW/>

===Breeding===
The only known blue seedeater nest was found in Mexico. It was a cup of coarse grass lined with finer grass placed in the fork of a slender branch. It contained two seedeater eggs and one of the [[brood parasite]] [[bronzed cowbird]] (''Molothrus aeneus'').<ref name=BLSE-BOW/>

==Status==
The [[IUCN]] has not assessed the blue seedeater.
